content.text:
https://docs.entire.io/guides/checkpoints/capture-checkpoints.md
→ https://docs.entire.io/guides/checkpoints/capture-checkpoints.md
Content-Type: text/markdown; charset=utf-8

> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.entire.io/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Capture Checkpoints

> Learn how Entire turns agent work into committed checkpoints.

Entire captures session context while you work, then creates a committed checkpoint when that context is linked to a Git commit.

## How capture works

Checkpoint capture has two parts:

1. [Agent hooks](/agents/overview) collect session context, including prompts, transcript, tool activity, changed files, and token usage when available.
2. Git hooks link that captured context to a commit by adding an [`Entire-Checkpoint` trailer](/glossary#entire-checkpoint).

After the commit, Entire stores checkpoint metadata on the [`entire/checkpoints/v1` branch](/security#where-your-data-lives). Your working branch stays clean.

## Set up capture for an agent

For a new repository, enable Entire with the agent you use:

```bash theme={null}
entire enable --agent AGENT
```

For a repository where Entire is already enabled, add another agent:

```bash theme={null}
entire agent add AGENT
```

You can see the supported built-in agents in [Agents](/agents/overview), or run:

```bash theme={null}
entire agent list
```

## Commit after agent work

To capture agent-assisted work in a checkpoint:

1. Start your agent from an Entire-enabled repository.
2. Ask the agent to make a change.
3. Stage and commit the change with Git.

```bash theme={null}
git add .
git commit -m "Describe the change"
```

If Entire detects recent agent work, it may ask whether to link the commit to the session context. Choose `yes` to create the checkpoint. Choose `always` if you want Entire to remember that preference for future matching commits.

```text theme={null}
Entire: Active Codex session detected
  Last prompt: Add a user settings page...

Link this commit to session context?
  [Y]es / [n]o / [a]lways (remember my choice):
```

## Link future commits automatically

When the "Link this commit to session context?" prompt appears during `git commit`, choosing `yes` or `no` only applies to that one commit.

To link future matching commits in this repository without being prompted during `git commit`, set `commit_linking` to `always`.

Set `commit_linking` in one of these files:

* `.entire/settings.local.json` if this preference should only apply to you
* `.entire/settings.json` if this should be the team setting for the repository

```json theme={null}
{
  "commit_linking": "always"
}
```

## Let the agent commit

Your agent can also create commits for you. For example, you might ask it to make a focused change and commit when it is done.

Entire still uses the Git hooks to link the commit to the captured session context. If the agent makes several commits in one session, each commit gets its own checkpoint.

## Capture multiple commits from one session

A single agent session can produce multiple checkpoints. This happens when a long session creates several commits, or when you split the agent's work into smaller commits yourself.

Each commit gets its own checkpoint ID. The checkpoints can share the same session context so you can still inspect the prompts, transcript, and work that led to each commit.

## Capture one checkpoint from multiple sessions

One checkpoint can also include more than one session. This happens when several agent sessions contribute to the work before you make a single commit.

In Entire.io, those sessions appear together inside the checkpoint's Sessions tab. The checkpoint diff shows the combined change, while [session details](/guides/sessions/sessions-in-entire-io) show the context behind each contribution.

## What does not create a checkpoint

Entire needs captured session context and a Git commit to create a normal committed checkpoint.

A checkpoint may not appear when:

* Entire has not been enabled in the repository
* agent hooks were not installed for the agent you used
* the agent did not change files in the repository
* no commit was created
* a GUI Git client could not find the `entire` command from its Git hooks

If a checkpoint is missing or did not link where you expected, see [Troubleshooting](/guides/checkpoints/troubleshooting).

content.text:
Direct answer: there isn't a command that backfills a checkpoint for those two commits, and I won't hand you one that quietly does nothing. Per the entire docs, a checkpoint is created from **captured agent-session context + a git commit linked by the git hook**. My commits ran in the Cowork sandbox, which left no session transcript on your Mac — so `entire import` (imports local transcripts) and `entire attach` (links an existing local session) have nothing on your machine to work from. The code itself is safe on `main` regardless; what can't be reconstructed is the session/prompt context behind it, and no command rebuilds that after the fact.

What's actually worth running — verify state and check for anything stuck:

```bash
cd ~/Developer/lab-memory
entire status              # current Entire state for this repo
entire checkpoint list     # checkpoints on the current branch (main)
entire doctor              # scan for stuck/problematic sessions, offer fixes
```

To make future repo work get checkpointed, the commit has to happen where an agent session was captured — i.e. run it through Claude Code (or another supported agent) on your Mac, then commit there. You can also stop the per-commit prompt by setting linking to always:

```bash
# in ~/Developer/lab-memory/.entire/settings.json  (team-wide)
# or .entire/settings.local.json  (just you; gitignored)
{
  "commit_linking": "always"
}
```

The practical rule: work I push from Cowork lands as plain git on `main` (safe, already on darthzen), but it won't carry an entire checkpoint. When you want the checkpoint trail, drive that work from Claude Code on your Mac and let its git hook link the commit.

content.subject: Refactor worker/ingest.py for list-routed, hierarchical SME memory
content.description: Add SME_ROUTES env (rootListId:collection). Fetch list tree + per-list membership via /api/v1/lists and /api/v1/lists/{id}/bookmarks. Compute each routed root's subtree; assign each bookmark to a target collection with list_id=deepest routed list (deepest-wins). Partition all bookmarks over one snapshot; reconcile each collection (lab_memory=complement, sme_* with list_id field) in a single pass. sme collections get a list_id VARCHAR field in ensure_collection.
content.activeForm: Refactoring the ingest worker for list routing

content.subject: Add ancestor-filtered recall to mcp retrieval.py + server.py
content.description: Add LIST_ID env (reader scope) + COLLECTION. Fetch /api/v1/lists once, build id->parentId, resolve LIST_ID to {self ∪ ancestors}. recall() filters list_id IN (chain) when scoped. Keep tag/expand. server.py recall tool gains optional list override; memory_status reports scope.
content.activeForm: Adding ancestor-filtered recall to the MCP server

content.subject: Add deploy manifests: SME_ROUTES env + sme reader deployment
content.description: Add SME_ROUTES to the existing ingest CronJob env. Copy mcp/k8s/deployment.yaml to an sme-salesengineer variant (COLLECTION=sme_salesengineer, LIST_ID=root, own service + ingress). SUSE BCI base per policy for any new custom image build.
content.activeForm: Writing deploy manifests for the SME reader

content.subject: Dry-run partition logic against live Karakeep, then present diffs for review
content.description: Exec the partition/subtree logic read-only against live Karakeep via the kubernetes MCP (no Milvus writes) to confirm routing. Present diffs for Rick to eyeball. Re-verify .entire/settings.json present before any commit; darthzen repo so entire artifacts stay committed and checkpoints/v1 pushes normally. Do not commit without approval.

content.text: Let me check exactly what `entire import` and `entire attach` can ingest before I answer — that determines whether replay is even useful.

https://docs.entire.io/cli-reference/import.md
→ https://docs.entire.io/cli-reference/import.md
Content-Type: text/markdown; charset=utf-8

> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.entire.io/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# import

> Import pre-existing local agent transcripts as read-only checkpoints.

Import pre-existing local agent transcripts for the current repository as read-only checkpoints.

```bash theme={null}
entire import AGENT [flags]
```

<Note>
  `entire import` is experimental and hidden from the top-level help in Entire CLI `0.7.8`. Run `entire import --help` to see the agents supported by your installed CLI version.
</Note>

## Supported agents

In Entire CLI `0.7.8`, import supports:

| Agent       | Command                         |
| ----------- | ------------------------------- |
| Claude Code | `entire import claude-code`     |
| Codex       | `entire import codex`           |
| Copilot CLI | `entire import copilot-cli`     |
| Cursor      | `entire import cursor`          |
| Factory     | `entire import factoryai-droid` |
| Gemini CLI  | `entire import gemini`          |
| Pi          | `entire import pi`              |

## Flags

| Flag               | Description                                               |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--dry-run`        | Report what would be imported without writing checkpoints |
| `--path DIRECTORY` | Override the transcript directory to import from          |
| `--session ID`     | Import only this session ID. Repeatable.                  |

## Examples

Check what would be imported:

```bash theme={null}
entire import claude-code --dry-run
```

Import Claude Code transcript history:

```bash theme={null}
entire import claude-code
```

Import a specific Codex session:

```bash theme={null}
entire import codex --session SESSION_ID
```

Import from a custom transcript directory:

```bash theme={null}
entire import claude-code --path TRANSCRIPT_DIRECTORY
```

## Behavior

`entire import` scans the agent's local transcript store for the current repository and imports history from the past month.

Imported checkpoints are:

* read-only
* searchable
* explainable
* idempotent, so already-imported turns are skipped
* not rewindable
* not linked to a Git commit

## Continue with

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="Import Past Sessions" icon="terminal" href="/guides/sessions/import-past-agent-history">
    Follow the guide for importing past local agent sessions.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Search" icon="sparkles" href="/cli-reference/search">
    Search imported checkpoints and other Entire history.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

content.text:
Good news: it's probably **not** gone, and reverting is the wrong move. `entire import` reads an agent's local transcript store directly — it doesn't care about git state, so replaying commits gains you nothing and would just risk the live deploy. What matters is that this Cowork session's transcript is sitting on your Mac, and entire can import Claude Code transcripts (Cowork runs on Claude Code).

This session's transcript lives under:

```
~/Library/Application Support/Claude/local-agent-mode-sessions/64847728-3b4e-478e-9992-0a205d5ccd93/177aa1a4-ea45-4161-84c6-884a889e0118/local_deecbc20-d022-4be9-9363-913691a53246/
```

Try importing it — `--dry-run` first, which writes nothing and just reports what it found:

```bash
cd ~/Developer/lab-memory
# find the actual transcript file(s) under the session folder:
find "$HOME/Library/Application Support/Claude/local-agent-mode-sessions/64847728-3b4e-478e-9992-0a205d5ccd93/177aa1a4-ea45-4161-84c6-884a889e0118/local_deecbc20-d022-4be9-9363-913691a53246" -name '*.jsonl'

# point entire's claude-code importer at that directory:
entire import claude-code --path "<dir containing the .jsonl>" --dry-run
```

If the dry-run reports sessions/turns, drop `--dry-run` to write them.

Three honest caveats:

- **Format compatibility is unverified.** Cowork is Claude-Code-based, so the JSONL is plausibly what `import claude-code` expects, but I can't confirm the schema matches from here. The `--dry-run` is your safe test — it either recognizes the transcript or it doesn't, with nothing written.
- **Imported checkpoints are read-only and not linked to the commits.** They capture the session context — prompts, transcript, reasoning — as searchable/explainable history, which is "the data" you care about. They won't be tied to `c97a3f6`/`b651a63`, but that linkage matters far less than the context itself.
- **Don't revert `main`.** The deployed system is live and the code is safe; a git replay recreates diffs, not session capture, and can't reconstruct the transcript anyway.

If entire can't parse the Cowork transcript, the content still isn't lost — I can export this whole session's transcript to a file in the repo (or your notes) so the design reasoning is preserved as plain text regardless of whether it becomes a formal checkpoint. Want me to do that as a safety net?

content.text:
That first path is the real prize — it's in Claude Code's native `.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/<session-uuid>.jsonl` layout, exactly what `entire import claude-code` reads. (The second, `audit.jsonl`, is Cowork's own audit log — ignore it.) Point `--path` at the directory containing that `.jsonl`:

```bash
cd ~/Developer/lab-memory
entire import claude-code \
  --path "/Users/rashford/Library/Application Support/Claude/local-agent-mode-sessions/64847728-3b4e-478e-9992-0a205d5ccd93/177aa1a4-ea45-4161-84c6-884a889e0118/local_deecbc20-d022-4be9-9363-913691a53246/.claude/projects/REDACTED" \
  --dry-run
```

If the dry-run reports the session and its turns, run it for real by dropping the last line:

```bash
cd ~/Developer/lab-memory
entire import claude-code \
  --path "/Users/rashford/Library/Application Support/Claude/local-agent-mode-sessions/64847728-3b4e-478e-9992-0a205d5ccd93/177aa1a4-ea45-4161-84c6-884a889e0118/local_deecbc20-d022-4be9-9363-913691a53246/.claude/projects/REDACTED"
```

If the dry-run finds nothing, two fallbacks:

- Target the session by ID (the `.jsonl` filename): add `--session d9667159-3e99-4b8a-bc4a-2c450bc1ae3f`.
- Point `--path` one level up at the `.claude/projects` directory, in case the importer expects the projects root rather than the per-project folder.

One caveat to expect: entire normally matches transcripts to the current repo by the recorded cwd, and this session's cwd is the Cowork `output` dir, not `~/Developer/lab-memory`. The explicit `--path` is meant to bypass that, but if the dry-run sees the file yet imports zero "for this repository," that cwd mismatch is why — and the imported checkpoints will be read-only and unlinked to your commits regardless. The `--dry-run` tells you safely either way.

lines: **SME memory LIVE 2026-07-15.** Hierarchical, list-routed SME layer on top of lab_memory (design in [[karakeep-list-api]]). Committed to darthzen/lab-memory (c97a3f6 feature + b651a63 entire artifacts), main + entire/checkpoints/v1 pushed. Worker (`worker/ingest.py`) gained `SME_ROUTES=rootListId:collection`: fetches list tree + per-list membership, routes each bookmark under a routed subtree into a per-domain collection stamped `list_id` (deepest-wins), single-pass reconcile of lab_memory (complement) + every SME collection from one snapshot (no comingling, no inter-job race), pre-seeds configured SME collections. MCP (`mcp/src/lab_memory_mcp/`) gained `LIST_ID` scope: cached parent-map, `ancestor_chain`, recall filters `list_id in {self+ancestors}` (downward inheritance), `save()` files into LIST_ID for deterministic write-routing. Reader-per-list topology: `mcp/k8s/sme-salesengineer.yaml` = parent reader (LIST_ID=ipnsyy1t55vakjtzkn25y1p0, scope {self}) + child reader salesengineer-manager (LIST_ID=uxupn8cpjd78dezx14fzl4sc, scope {child,parent}), both COLLECTION=sme_salesengineer. DEPLOYED via kubernetes MCP: ConfigMaps lab-memory-ingest-script + lab-memory-mcp-src updated from repo, cronjob patched with SME_ROUTES, both SME reader Deployments/Services/Ingresses up, base lab-memory-mcp restarted. Verified: manual ingest created `sme_salesengineer` (1024-d COSINE, list_id field, rows=0), lab_memory untouched (540 chunks). DNS live for mcp-sme-salesengineer + mcp-sme-salesengineer-manager (certs will issue via letsencrypt-dns). Adding an SME domain = new top-level Karakeep list + SME_ROUTES entry + reader manifest.

-**Gotchas learned 2026-07-15:** (1) The `~/Developer` mount in the Cowork sandbox blocks `unlink()` on .git files — stale `*.lock` files can't be `rm`'d; rename them aside (`mv x.lock x.lock.stale`) since rename IS permitted, else git commits fail on lock creation. (2) The lab-memory remote is now SSH (git@github.com:darthzen/lab-memory.git) but the sandbox has no SSH host key — push over HTTPS with the darthzen PAT instead. (3) entire CLI is NOT installed in the Cowork sandbox, so the `.claude/settings.json` git hook self-skips ("Entire CLI enabled but not on PATH") on sandbox commits — commits are plain git (safe, pushed) but generate no entire checkpoints; commit from a machine with entire installed for checkpoint coverage.

content.subject: Refactor worker/ingest.py for list-routed, hierarchical SME memory
content.description: Add SME_ROUTES env (rootListId:collection). Fetch list tree + per-list membership via /api/v1/lists and /api/v1/lists/{id}/bookmarks. Compute each routed root's subtree; assign each bookmark to a target collection with list_id=deepest routed list (deepest-wins). Partition all bookmarks over one snapshot; reconcile each collection (lab_memory=complement, sme_* with list_id field) in a single pass. sme collections get a list_id VARCHAR field in ensure_collection.
content.activeForm: Refactoring the ingest worker for list routing

content.subject: Add ancestor-filtered recall to mcp retrieval.py + server.py
content.description: Add LIST_ID env (reader scope) + COLLECTION. Fetch /api/v1/lists once, build id->parentId, resolve LIST_ID to {self ∪ ancestors}. recall() filters list_id IN (chain) when scoped. Keep tag/expand. server.py recall tool gains optional list override; memory_status reports scope.

content.text:
Full loop closed. To recap where things landed:

- **SME memory built and live** — list-routed, hierarchical, inheritance-at-recall. `sme_salesengineer` collection created, both parent and child readers running, `lab_memory` untouched.
- **Committed and pushed** to darthzen/lab-memory (`c97a3f6` + `b651a63`), `main` and `entire/checkpoints/v1` both up.
- **Session context recovered** into entire via `import claude-code`.

Whenever you file content into the `salesengineer` list (or the `salesengineer-manager` child), the next ingest cycle routes it into the SME collection with its `list_id`, and the child reader returns it inheriting the parent. Adding a future SME domain is just: new top-level Karakeep list → `SME_ROUTES` entry → reader manifest.

content.text: Let me confirm the canonical mechanism from the entire docs rather than guess at it.

https://docs.entire.io/guides/configuration/git-hooks.md
→ https://docs.entire.io/guides/configuration/git-hooks.md
Content-Type: text/markdown; charset=utf-8

> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.entire.io/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Git Hooks

> Configure the Git hooks Entire uses to capture and sync checkpoint data.

Use this page when `git commit` or `git push` is not creating, linking, or syncing checkpoint data.

Entire uses Git hooks to run during normal Git commands:

* `git commit` links captured agent work to the commit and creates an `Entire-Checkpoint` trailer.
* `git push` syncs checkpoint data after your code push.

If those behaviors do not happen, Git may not be running Entire's hooks, or the hook may not be able to find the `entire` command.

## Start here if

* a commit did not create a checkpoint
* a commit is missing an `Entire-Checkpoint` trailer
* checkpoint data is not syncing after `git push`
* commits work from Terminal, but not from a GUI Git client
* Git says it cannot find the `entire` command
* hooks were removed, overwritten, or need to be reinstalled
* the `entire` binary moved on your machine
* setup needs to run without prompts

<Note>
  Git hooks are different from [agent hooks](/guides/configuration/agent-hooks).
  Agent hooks capture the AI session while you work; Git hooks connect that
  captured session to commits and pushes.
</Note>

## Install Git hooks

For a new repository, run `entire enable` to install the Git hooks Entire needs for commit and push behavior:

```bash theme={null}
entire enable
```

## Reinstall Git hooks

If hooks were removed, overwritten, or stopped running, reinstall them with:

```bash theme={null}
entire configure --force
```

## GUI Git clients

Terminal commits can work while GUI commits fail because apps like Xcode, Tower, GitKraken, and Sourcetree may not load your shell `$PATH`.

Configure hooks with the full `entire` binary path:

```bash theme={null}
entire configure --local --absolute-git-hook-path
```

During initial setup, use the same option with `entire enable`:

```bash theme={null}
entire enable --local --absolute-git-hook-path
```

This embeds the full path to the `entire` binary directly in the Git hooks:

```sh theme={null}
# Default
entire hooks git post-commit

# With --absolute-git-hook-path
'/Users/you/go/bin/entire' hooks git post-commit
```

The setting is written to `.entire/settings.local.json` when you pass `--local`:

```json .entire/settings.local.json theme={null}
{
  "absolute_git_hook_path": true
}
```

Absolute Git hook paths are machine-specific. If you move the `entire` binary, rerun:

```bash theme={null}
entire configure --local --absolute-git-hook-path
```

## Use non-interactive setup

For scripts, CI, or bootstrap flows, run setup in non-interactive mode:

```bash theme={null}
entire enable --yes
```

To select an agent without prompts:

```bash theme={null}
entire enable --yes --agent claude-code
```

## Verify Git hooks

After changing hook setup, check the repository status and test the Git client you actually use:

```bash theme={null}
entire status --detailed
```

Then make a test commit and push. If no checkpoint appears, run [checkpoint troubleshooting](/guides/checkpoints/troubleshooting).

content.text:
https://docs.entire.io/guides/checkpoints/store-checkpoints-with-your-code.md
→ https://docs.entire.io/guides/checkpoints/store-checkpoints-with-your-code.md
Content-Type: text/markdown; charset=utf-8

> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.entire.io/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Store Checkpoints with Your Code

> Keep checkpoint history in the same Git repository as the code it describes.

By default, Entire stores checkpoint data in the same Git repository as your code. This keeps the commit, checkpoint, and agent context together while leaving your normal Git history clean.

## Choose a checkpoint backend

The checkpoint backend controls how Entire organizes checkpoint data inside Git.

| Backend                    | How it stores checkpoints                                           | Checkpoint ID                                                        |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Branch-based (default)** | Stores all checkpoints on the shared `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch | 12-character hexadecimal ID, such as `a3b2c4d5e6f7`                  |
| **Ref-based**              | Stores each checkpoint in its own Git ref                           | 26-character, time-sortable ID, such as `01KVBJCWYA4YW6J5M9GP655HZN` |

### Branch-based storage

Branch-based storage requires no additional configuration. Entire writes checkpoint data to `entire/checkpoints/v1` and pushes that branch alongside your code.

To switch an existing repository back to branch-based storage, run:

```bash theme={null}
entire configure --checkpoint-backend branch
```

### Ref-based storage

Ref-based storage lets Entire push and fetch checkpoints independently instead of updating one shared branch. This keeps checkpoint operations fast as agent history grows and lets multiple agents save checkpoints at the same time without competing to update the same branch.

For a new repository, run:

```bash theme={null}
entire enable --checkpoint-backend refs
```

For an existing Entire-enabled repository, run:

```bash theme={null}
entire configure --checkpoint-backend refs
```

Changing the backend affects new checkpoints. Existing checkpoints remain on `entire/checkpoints/v1` and continue to work alongside new ref-based checkpoints. Your workflow and Entire commands stay the same.

<Note>
  Add `--local` to a configuration command when the backend choice should apply only to your clone. See [Where storage settings are saved](/guides/checkpoints/store-checkpoint-data#where-storage-settings-are-saved).
</Note>

content.text:
The imported checkpoints live on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch, and entire's push hook syncs that branch when you push code. Two ways to get them up:

**Direct (most reliable, since the import isn't tied to a new commit):**

```bash
cd ~/Developer/lab-memory
git push origin entire/checkpoints/v1
```

**Or let the hook carry it** — entire's git post-push hook syncs checkpoints whenever you push code, so a normal `git push origin main` publishes the branch alongside any code changes. But with nothing new on `main`, the explicit branch push above is the clean route.

On your Mac this just works over the SSH remote — no PAT needed (that was only because the Cowork sandbox had no SSH key). It'll be a fast-forward: your local branch has the newly imported checkpoints on top of the `7c2ded6` init commit that's already on darthzen.

Verify it synced:

```bash
entire status --detailed
```

That's it — branch-based storage (your default) keeps checkpoint history in the same repo and pushes it alongside your code, so once that branch is up, the recovered session context is on GitHub with everything else.

lastPrompt: What do I need to do to get the mcp servers for salesengineer and salesengineer-manager running?

content.text:
Everything server-side is live — both return HTTP 406 to a plain GET, which is the correct response from a streamable-http MCP server (it wants the SSE `Accept` header), so TLS, ingress, service, and the MCP process are all wired end to end, same as your working `mcp-memory` connector.

So there's nothing left to deploy. To make them **accessible in Claude**, register each as a custom connector — the same flow you used for `mcp-memory.ash4d.com`:

- **Settings → Connectors → Add custom connector** (remote MCP server), then name + URL:
  - `SME: Sales Engineer` → `https://mcp-sme-salesengineer.ash4d.com/mcp`
  - `SME: Sales Engineer Manager` → `https://mcp-sme-salesengineer-manager.ash4d.com/mcp`

Both are streamable-http, no auth, so it's just name + URL — no token step. Each exposes `recall`, `save`, and `memory_status`; the manager one inherits the parent's knowledge via its ancestor scope, the parent one sees only its own.

Two things to expect:

- **Recall returns empty until you file content.** `sme_salesengineer` exists but has 0 rows. Drop a bookmark into the `salesengineer` (or `salesengineer-manager`) list, and the next ingest cycle (≤15 min) makes it recallable — or use the connector's `save` tool, which files into the right list automatically.
- **These endpoints are unauthenticated** (same posture as `mcp-memory` — your open T-014). Anyone who can reach `*.ash4d.com` on your network can query or `save`. Fine for the lab; worth locking down before any of these leave the LAN.

Once you've added the two connectors, ask Claude to `recall` against them (or `memory_status` for a quick health check) to confirm they're live in the client.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about configuring remote MCP servers with `claude_desktop_config.json`:

## Key Findings

When configuring MCP servers via JSON in .mcp.json, ~/.claude.json, or claude mcp add-json, the type field accepts streamable-http as an alias for http.

### Configuration with mcp-remote

Claude Desktop's local config schema validates stdio servers only. The url and type: "http" fields you see in Claude Code docs and on every "remote MCP" landing page are parsed by a different client.

For Claude Desktop specifically, two fixes work today: register the remote server as a Custom Connector in Settings, or wrap it with the mcp-remote stdio bridge in claude_desktop_config.json.

### Example Configuration

An example uses mcp-remote with: { "mcpServers": { "asana": { "command": "npx", "args": ["mcp-remote", "https://mcp.asana.com/sse"] } } }

### Streamable HTTP Standard (2026)

The old HTTP+SSE (Server-Sent Events) transport has been replaced by Streamable HTTP, introduced in the November 2025 MCP spec. This is now the standard for any MCP server you want to deploy as a remote, cloud-hosted service. It uses a single HTTPS endpoint: POST for JSON-RPC requests and an optional GET endpoint for streaming.

### Location of Configuration File

Claude Desktop stores the config file at: macOS: `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json` or Windows: `%APPDATA%\\Claude\\claude_desktop_config.json`


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

## Key Findings

### Configuration with mcp-remote

Claude Desktop's local config schema validates stdio servers only. The url and type: "http" fields you see in Claude Code docs and on every "remote MCP" landing page are parsed by a different client.

For Claude Desktop specifically, two fixes work today: register the remote server as a Custom Connector in Settings, or wrap it with the mcp-remote stdio bridge in claude_desktop_config.json.

### Example Configuration

An example uses mcp-remote with: { "mcpServers": { "asana": { "command": "npx", "args": ["mcp-remote", "https://mcp.asana.com/sse"] } } }

### Streamable HTTP Standard (2026)

The old HTTP+SSE (Server-Sent Events) transport has been replaced by Streamable HTTP, introduced in the November 2025 MCP spec. This is now the standard for any MCP server you want to deploy as a remote, cloud-hosted service. It uses a single HTTPS endpoint: POST for JSON-RPC requests and an optional GET endpoint for streaming.

lastPrompt: I entered a blog post on meddic to the salesengineer list, so it should show up on both mcp servers once the cron job ingests the data.

content.text:
Confirmed working exactly as designed. Both connectors return the MEDDIC post (Atlassian article, bookmark `x4p2o6vzy4sy2eea213bn4cp`, 8 chunks, score ~0.70):

- **`sme-salesengineer`** (parent) — returns it directly; it's in its own list.
- **`sme-salesengineer-manager`** (child) — returns the same doc, pulled in through the ancestor filter. That's the inheritance proving itself: parent content, visible to the child, with no copy.
- **`lab_memory` untouched** — 73 docs, `delete=0`. The post went straight to `sme_salesengineer` and never entered the general collection, so no comingling even for one cycle.

So the full loop is live: file into a Karakeep list → cron routes it (≤15 min, or on-demand like just now) → the scoped readers serve it, children inheriting parents. In your Claude Desktop the same `recall` will return it now that the connectors are registered.

The one thing to keep in mind going forward: put shared sales-engineering material in the `salesengineer` parent so every sub-domain inherits it, and reserve the `salesengineer-manager` child for manager-specific content that shouldn't flow back up to the parent reader.

      "title": "popovich_leadership",
      "snippet": "# Gregg Popovich: A Leadership Overview

**Scope note:** Popovich deliberately avoids discussing his own leadership philosophy in the abstract. This document is built from documented facts — his record, his decisions, direct quotes, and firsthand accounts from players, assistants, and reporters — with inferences clearly labeled as such. Facts are stated bluntly; inference sections are marked **[INFERENCE]**.

---

## 1. Background (the raw material of the style)

- **Born 1949, East Chicago, Indiana.** Working-class steel town. Serbian father, Croatian mother — R.C. Buford has joked that Pop carries "the Serbo-Croatian conflict" internally.
- **U.S. Air Force Academy**, degree in **Soviet Studies**. Captain of the basketball team.
- **Five years active duty**, toured Eastern Europe and the USSR with the Armed Forces basketball team. Seriously considered a career in the **CIA**.
- **Pomona-Pitzer (Division III), 1979–1988.** Head coach at a school with no athletic scholarships and, initially, no gym worth the name. Lived in a student dorm with his wife and kids his first year as a faculty resident. Took a **sabbatical year to study under Dean Smith (UNC) and Larry Brown (Kansas)** — a working coach voluntarily going back to school mid-career.
- **NBA path:** Assistant under Larry Brown (Spurs, 1988–92), then Don Nelson (Warriors, 1992–94). Returned to San Antonio as **GM in 1994**. In December 1996, with the team 3–15, he **fired Bob Hill and named himself head coach** — one of the most nakedly self-interested moves of his career, and worth remembering when the humility narrative gets too tidy.
- **The record:** 5 NBA championships (1999, 2003, 2005, 2007, 2014). 22 consecutive winning seasons — an NBA record. Most wins by any coach in NBA history. 3× Coach of the Year. Olympic gold with Team USA (Tokyo 2021). Hall of Fame, 2023.
- **Current status:** Suffered a stroke in November 2024, stepped down as head coach in May 2025 after 29 seasons, and",
      "citation": "https://karakeep.ash4d.com/dashboard/preview/ze9bfp1dx9awgeftr6fklr4s",
      "source_url": "https://karakeep.ash4d.com/dashboard/preview/ze9bfp1dx9awgeftr6fklr4s",
      "tags": "NBA,Leadership,Gregg Popovich,Coaching Philosophy,Sports Management,Organizational Culture",
      "score": 0.7189,
      "chunk": 0
    },
    {
      "title": "popovich_leadership",
      "snippet": "nely used press conferences to credit opponents, role players, and assistants, and deflected credit from himself with near-total consistency.

### 2.5 Delegation and succession as a first-class output

- **The coaching tree is arguably his greatest artifact:** Mike Budenholzer, Brett Brown, Ime Udoka, Will Hardy, Taylor Jenkins, Monty Williams, James Borrego, Jacque Vaughn, Mike Brown, and current Spurs coach Mitch Johnson all came through his staff. Multiple won Coach of the Year or reached Finals elsewhere.
- **Becky Hammon, 2014:** first full-time female assistant coach in NBA history — hired on merit after he watched her basketball mind up close, not as a PR exercise. She ran the Spurs' 2015 Summer League team to a title and acted as head coach when he was ejected in 2020, another first.
- Assistants ran film sessions and had genuine authority in practice. Popovich is on record crediting his own development to Larry Brown and Dean Smith giving him real responsibility, and he replicated that structure.
- **The silent timeout** became a signature: during some timeouts he would stand back and let the players sort it out among themselves, occasionally telling them some version of "I've got nothing for you — you figure it out." Player self-organization was treated as a trainable skill, deliberately practiced in low-stakes moments so it existed in high-stakes ones.

### 2.6 Adaptability underneath apparent constancy

- The Spurs' *values* stayed fixed for 29 years; the *system* was rebuilt at least three times:
  - **1997–2007:** Twin Towers, defense-first, post-centric grind built around Robinson and Duncan.
  - **~2010–2016:** As Duncan aged and rules changed, Popovich rebuilt the offense into the pace-and-movement "beautiful game" — high assist rates, corner threes, constant ball and body movement. The 2014 Finals performance is widely considered one of the most aesthetically complete team performances in league history.
  - **2023–present:** Full rebui",

      "title": "popovich_leadership",
      "snippet": "# Gregg Popovich: A Leadership Overview

**Scope note:** Popovich deliberately avoids discussing his own leadership philosophy in the abstract. This document is built from documented facts — his record, his decisions, direct quotes, and firsthand accounts from players, assistants, and reporters — with inferences clearly labeled as such. Facts are stated bluntly; inference sections are marked **[INFERENCE]**.

---

## 1. Background (the raw material of the style)

- **Born 1949, East Chicago, Indiana.** Working-class steel town. Serbian father, Croatian mother — R.C. Buford has joked that Pop carries "the Serbo-Croatian conflict" internally.
- **U.S. Air Force Academy**, degree in **Soviet Studies**. Captain of the basketball team.
- **Five years active duty**, toured Eastern Europe and the USSR with the Armed Forces basketball team. Seriously considered a career in the **CIA**.
- **Pomona-Pitzer (Division III), 1979–1988.** Head coach at a school with no athletic scholarships and, initially, no gym worth the name. Lived in a student dorm with his wife and kids his first year as a faculty resident. Took a **sabbatical year to study under Dean Smith (UNC) and Larry Brown (Kansas)** — a working coach voluntarily going back to school mid-career.
- **NBA path:** Assistant under Larry Brown (Spurs, 1988–92), then Don Nelson (Warriors, 1992–94). Returned to San Antonio as **GM in 1994**. In December 1996, with the team 3–15, he **fired Bob Hill and named himself head coach** — one of the most nakedly self-interested moves of his career, and worth remembering when the humility narrative gets too tidy.
- **The record:** 5 NBA championships (1999, 2003, 2005, 2007, 2014). 22 consecutive winning seasons — an NBA record. Most wins by any coach in NBA history. 3× Coach of the Year. Olympic gold with Team USA (Tokyo 2021). Hall of Fame, 2023.
- **Current status:** Suffered a stroke in November 2024, stepped down as head coach in May 2025 after 29 seasons, and",

Multiple won Coach of the Year or reached Finals elsewhere.
- **Becky Hammon, 2014:** first full-time female assistant coach in NBA history — hired on merit after he watched her basketball mind up close, not as a PR exercise. She ran the Spurs' 2015 Summer League team to a title and acted as head coach when he was ejected in 2020, another first.
- Assistants ran film sessions and had genuine authority in practice. Popovich is on record crediting his own development to Larry Brown and Dean Smith giving him real responsibility, and he replicated that structure.
- **The silent timeout** became a signature: during some timeouts he would stand back and let the players sort it out among themselves, occasionally telling them some version of "I've got nothing for you — you figure it out." Player self-organization was treated as a trainable skill, deliberately practiced in low-stakes moments so it existed in high-stakes ones.

### 2.6 Adaptability underneath apparent constancy

- The Spurs' *values* stayed fixed for 29 years; the *system* was rebuilt at least three times:
  - **1997–2007:** Twin Towers, defense-first, post-centric grind built around Robinson and Duncan.
  - **~2010–2016:** As Duncan aged and rules changed, Popovich rebuilt the offense into the pace-and-movement "beautiful game" — high assist rates, corner threes, constant ball and body movement. The 2014 Finals performance is widely considered one of the most aesthetically complete team performances in league history.

 5. Identify the pain points 



 These are the underlying motivations that drive a buyer to seek a solution. The focus should be on moving beyond surface-level issues to understand the core challenges that create urgency. 



 Sales representatives need to link the value proposition directly to these uncovered pain points. For example, they can ask, “How is this issue impacting your department’s goals or overall business performance?” or “What are the financial or operational consequences if this problem remains unsolved?”

Separation confirmed — the hierarchy behaves exactly as designed:

| Query: "Gregg Popovich…" | Parent reader (`sme-salesengineer`) | Child reader (`sme-salesengineer-manager`) |
|---|---|---|
| Popovich doc (child list) | **Not returned** | **Returned, 0.72 / 0.63** |
| MEDDIC doc (parent list) | Returned (0.20, only thing in scope) | Returned (0.20, inherited) |

- **Upward isolation holds:** the parent reader never surfaces the Popovich doc — child-list content is invisible to the parent. The MEDDIC hits it returns are just its own in-scope doc showing up at low relevance because vector search always returns nearest neighbors.
- **Downward inheritance holds:** the child reader returns its own Popovich content at high relevance *and* the inherited MEDDIC doc from the parent.

That's the full validation of the design: `salesengineer-manager` sees everything in `salesengineer` plus its own, while `salesengineer` stays walled off from the manager-only material. Routing, `list_id` stamping, and the ancestor filter are all working against real content on the live connectors.

One optional refinement worth noting: because semantic search always returns nearest neighbors, a reader with little in scope will surface low-relevance hits (the parent's 0.20 MEDDIC results on a Popovich query). If you'd rather it return nothing below a relevance floor, I can add a minimum-score cutoff to `recall` — say, drop anything under ~0.3. Small change, your call.

content.text: Good instinct to not eyeball it — let me characterize the actual score distribution on your live data before recommending a floor, rather than pull 0.3 out of the air. The parent reader has exactly one doc (MEDDIC), so any unrelated query against it returns that doc at pure-noise score — that gives me the noise ceiling for this embedding model directly. And a few real-but-differently-worded queries show how low a *legitimate* hit can score.

      "snippet": "a floor requirement.** Players not selected for the culture (or acquired mid-career without the trust runway) sometimes experienced the candor as pure harshness. The system's dependence on pre-screening is a limitation, not just a feature.
- **The self-promotion origin.** He got the job by firing his coach and installing himself while GM. The servant-leader reputation was built *after* a decidedly non-servant power move.
- **Structural advantages that don't transfer:** drafting Duncan first overall in 1997, near-unfireable job security, ownership that never meddled, and a small market with low media pressure. The style is real, but it grew in unusually favorable soil.

---

## 5. Synthesis: the operating model in one place

Stated as a system rather than a slogan list:

1. **Screen for coachability and low ego before anything else.** Development capacity is bounded by selection quality.
2. **Bank relational capital deliberately, in advance, at personal cost.** Dinners, cultural fluency, knowing families — the trust account gets funded before withdrawals begin.
3. **Spend that capital on maximum candor, aimed hardest at the most senior people.** Criticizing the star first makes criticism safe for everyone.
4. **Hold values constant; treat systems and schemes as fully disposable.** Adapt the *how* relentlessly, never the *what matters*.
5. **Optimize for the long horizon even when it costs in the short term** — rest, fines, rebuild years, developing assistants who will leave.
6. **Make succession an explicit output.** The coaching tree and the Hammon hire were investments in people whose success would accrue elsewhere.
7. **Treat people as whole humans on company time.** Curiosity about their lives is part of the job, not a distraction from it.
8. **Deflect credit outward with total consistency.** It compounds trust and keeps the ego screen credible.

**[INFERENCE — the one-sentence version]** Popovich's style is high standards fused with high, pre-paid care, ap",

      "source_url": "https://karakeep.ash4d.com/dashboard/preview/ze9bfp1dx9awgeftr6fklr4s",
      "tags": "NBA,Leadership,Gregg Popovich,Coaching Philosophy,Sports Management,Organizational Culture",

    },
    {
      "title": "popovich_leadership",
      "snippet": "e reputation for abrasiveness with none of the results.

### 2.3 Relationships as infrastructure, built deliberately and off the clock

This is the most heavily documented and most distinctive pillar.

- **The dinners.** Popovich's team dinners are legendary and extensively reported: he personally selected restaurants in every road city, curated the wine (he is a serious oenophile and part-owner of a winery), spent enormous sums, and after championships had **menus engraved as keepsakes**. Attendance mixed players, coaches, and staff. Business talk was largely absent.
- **Cultural homework.** With the most international roster pipeline in the league (Ginóbili, Parker, Splitter, Mills, Belinelli, and many others), Popovich learned about each player's home country — its politics, food, history — and engaged them on it. The New York Times documented the Spurs' locker room operating in multiple languages as a genuine organizational feature. He reportedly greeted players in their native languages and asked pointed questions about elections back home.
- **Knowing the whole person.** Players across three decades describe the same pattern: Popovich knew their parents' names, their kids' birthdays, what was going on in their lives outside basketball, and he checked in on those things before talking basketball.
- **The famous formulation** (paraphrased consistently by former players and assistants): he could coach players brutally hard **because they already knew, from a thousand off-court interactions, that he cared about them as people**. The care was banked in advance; the candor drew against that balance.

**[INFERENCE]** The dinners and cultural fluency read as personality — and partly are — but the consistency, expense, and universality across 29 years mark them as deliberate trust engineering. He built the relational capital *before* he needed it, at high cost, on his own time. Most leaders attempt candor first and relationship-building as damage control after",

content.subject: Dry-run partition logic against live Karakeep, then present diffs for review
content.description: Exec the partition/subtree logic read-only against live Karakeep via the kubernetes MCP (no Milvus writes) to confirm routing. Present diffs for Rick to eyeball. Re-verify .entire/settings.json present before any commit; darthzen repo so entire artifacts stay committed and checkpoints/v1 pushes normally. Do not commit without approval.

content.text:
Great question to ask empirically, because the data says **0.3 is too high — it would cut genuinely relevant results.** Here's what your live corpus actually scores:

| Query type | Query | Score |
|---|---|---|
| Noise | "photosynthesis chloroplast…" → MEDDIC | 0.04 |
| Noise | "sourdough bread…" → MEDDIC | 0.11 |
| Noise | "orbital mechanics…" → MEDDIC | 0.12 |
| Noise (conceptual bleed) | "Gregg Popovich…" → MEDDIC (parent) | 0.20 |
| **Legit, paraphrased** | "adapting your system while keeping values constant" → Popovich | **0.26–0.29** |
| **Legit, paraphrased** | "team culture through delegation" → Popovich | **0.32–0.36** |
| Legit, close wording | "qualifying deals, economic buyer" → MEDDIC | 0.53 |
| Legit, direct | exact-topic MEDDIC / Popovich | 0.63–0.72 |

The key finding: **your noise tops out around 0.20, but genuinely on-topic paraphrased queries drop to 0.26.** That "adapting your system while keeping values constant" query is a bullseye match for the Popovich doc (it's literally section 4 of that document) — and it only scored 0.26. A 0.3 floor kills it.

`snowflake-arctic-embed2` compresses its cosine range, so good matches sit lower than the 0.7+ you'd expect from other models. The right floor threads the gap between noise (≤0.20) and weakest-legit (~0.26):

- **0.3 → too aggressive**, cuts real hits.
- **~0.25 → the sweet spot**: above the 0.20 conceptual-bleed noise, keeps the 0.26 paraphrase hits (barely).

Two caveats: it's a 2-doc sample, and the noise/legit bands nearly touch, so no single number is perfect. I'd implement it as a **tunable env var** (default 0.25) so you can adjust per reader without a code change, and pair it with query expansion later if paraphrased recall needs a lift. Let me build it that way.

input.new_string:
COLLECTION = os.environ.get("COLLECTION", "lab_memory")
# Relevance floor: drop hits whose COSINE score is below this. On arctic-embed2
# (compressed score range) live data shows noise topping out ~0.20 while weakest
# genuine paraphrase matches land ~0.26, so 0.25 cuts junk without losing real
# hits. Tunable per reader via env; 0 disables the floor.
MIN_SCORE = float(os.environ.get("RECALL_MIN_SCORE", "0.25"))

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Recall + save core for lab-memory. Shared by the MCP server and the eval harness.

Retrieval path: query -> (optional llama3.1 expansion) -> arctic-embed2 with the
QUERY_PREFIX -> Milvus COSINE search -> snippets with Karakeep citations.
Documents were embedded raw at ingest time; only queries carry the prefix.
"""

import os
from dataclasses import dataclass

import requests
from pymilvus import MilvusClient

OLLAMA_URL = os.environ.get("OLLAMA_URL", "http://ollama.ai.svc.cluster.local:11434")
EMBED_MODEL = os.environ.get("EMBED_MODEL", "snowflake-arctic-embed2")
EXPAND_MODEL = os.environ.get("EXPAND_MODEL", "llama3.1")
MILVUS_URI = os.environ.get("MILVUS_URI", "http://milvus.ai.svc.cluster.local:19530")
COLLECTION = os.environ.get("COLLECTION", "lab_memory")
# When set, this reader is scoped to a Karakeep list: recall widens to the list's
# ancestor chain ({self + parents}) so a child SME list inherits its parents'
# knowledge. Empty = a plain collection reader (e.g. lab_memory), no list filter.
LIST_ID = os.environ.get("LIST_ID", "").strip()
KARAKEEP_API_ADDR = os.environ.get("KARAKEEP_API_ADDR", "http://karakeep.lab-memory.svc.cluster.local:3000")
KARAKEEP_API_KEY = os.environ.get("KARAKEEP_API_KEY", "")
PUBLIC_ADDR = os.environ.get("PUBLIC_ADDR", "https://karakeep.ash4d.com").rstrip("/")
QUERY_PREFIX = "query: "

_client = None
_parent_cache = None  # {list_id: parentId}, fetched once per process


def milvus() -> MilvusClient:
    global _client
    if _client is None:
        _client = MilvusClient(uri=MILVUS_URI)
        _client.load_collection(COLLECTION)
    return _client


@dataclass
class Hit:
    title: str
    snippet: str
    citation: str
    source_url: str
    tags: str
    score: float
    chunk_ix: int
    karakeep_id: str


def expand_query(query: str, timeout: int = 30) -> str:
    """Widen the query with synonyms via llama3.1. Falls back to the raw query."""
    prompt = (
        "Rewrite this search query for a semantic search engine over technical "
        "documents. Keep every proper noun. Add likely synonyms and the words a "
        "manual would use. Answer with the rewritten query only, one line.\n\n"
        f"Query: {query}"
    )
    try:
        r = requests.post(
            f"{OLLAMA_URL}/api/generate",
            json={"model": EXPAND_MODEL, "prompt": prompt, "stream": False, "options": {"temperature": 0}},
            timeout=timeout,
        )
        r.raise_for_status()
        out = (r.json().get("response") or "").strip().splitlines()
        text = out[0].strip() if out else ""
        return f"{query} {text}".strip() if text else query
    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 -- expansion is best-effort
        return query


def embed_query(text: str) -> list:
    r = requests.post(
        f"{OLLAMA_URL}/api/embed",
        json={"model": EMBED_MODEL, "input": [QUERY_PREFIX + text]},
        timeout=120,
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    vectors = r.json().get("embeddings")
    if not vectors:
        raise RuntimeError(f"no embedding returned by {EMBED_MODEL}")
    return vectors[0]


def _parent_map() -> dict:
    """{list_id: parentId} for every Karakeep list, fetched once and cached.

    Karakeep list membership does not propagate to parents, so parent docs live
    only under their own list_id; inheritance is done here at query time.
    """
    global _parent_cache
    if _parent_cache is None:
        headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {KARAKEEP_API_KEY}"}
        parents, cursor = {}, None
        while True:
            params = {"limit": 100}
            if cursor:
                params["cursor"] = cursor
            r = requests.get(f"{KARAKEEP_API_ADDR}/api/v1/lists", params=params, headers=headers, timeout=30)
            r.raise_for_status()
            data = r.json()
            for L in data.get("lists", []):
                parents[L["id"]] = L.get("parentId")
            cursor = data.get("nextCursor")
            if not cursor:
                break
        _parent_cache = parents
    return _parent_cache


def ancestor_chain(list_id: str) -> list:
    """[list_id, parent, grandparent, ...] -- the scope a child inherits from."""
    chain, seen, cur = [], set(), list_id
    parents = _parent_map()
    while cur and cur not in seen:
        chain.append(cur)
        seen.add(cur)
        cur = parents.get(cur)
    return chain


def _scope_filter() -> str:
    """Milvus filter restricting recall to this reader's list + its ancestors."""
    if not LIST_ID:
        return ""
    ids = ancestor_chain(LIST_ID)
    quoted = ", ".join(f'"{i}"' for i in ids)
    return f"list_id in [{quoted}]"


def recall(query: str, k: int = 5, tag: str = "", expand: bool = False, per_doc: int = 2) -> list:
    """Semantic search over the shelf. `tag` filters on Karakeep tags (substring match).

    A list-scoped reader (LIST_ID set) also restricts results to its list plus all
    ancestor lists, so a child inherits parent knowledge without any data copy.
    """
    text = expand_query(query) if expand else query
    vector = embed_query(text)
    clauses = [c for c in (_scope_filter(), f'tags like "%{tag}%"' if tag else "") if c]
    flt = " and ".join(clauses)
    res = milvus().search(
        collection_name=COLLECTION,
        data=[vector],
        limit=max(k * 4, 12),
        filter=flt,
        output_fields=["karakeep_id", "chunk_ix", "title", "url", "preview_url", "tags", "preview"],
        search_params={"metric_type": "COSINE"},
    )
    hits = []
    seen = {}
    for match in res[0]:
        e = match["entity"]
        kid = e["karakeep_id"]
        if seen.get(kid, 0) >= per_doc:
            continue
        seen[kid] = seen.get(kid, 0) + 1
        hits.append(
            Hit(
                title=e["title"],
                snippet=e["preview"],
                # built at query time, never read from the stored field: changing
                # PUBLIC_ADDR (e.g. http -> https) must not require re-embedding the corpus
                citation=f"{PUBLIC_ADDR}/dashboard/preview/{kid}",
                source_url=e["url"],
                tags=e["tags"],
                score=round(float(match["distance"]), 4),
                chunk_ix=int(e["chunk_ix"]),
                karakeep_id=kid,
            )
        )
        if len(hits) >= k:
            break
    return hits


def save(tags: list, url: str = "", text: str = "", title: str = "") -> dict:
    """Create a Karakeep bookmark. Provenance tag `claude-sourced` is always added
    (docs/conventions.md); the ingest CronJob embeds it on the next cycle."""
    if not KARAKEEP_API_KEY:
        raise RuntimeError("KARAKEEP_API_KEY not set")
    if bool(url) == bool(text):
        raise ValueError("pass exactly one of url or text")
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {KARAKEEP_API_KEY}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}
    body = {"type": "link", "url": url} if url else {"type": "text", "text": text}
    if title:
        body["title"] = title
    r = requests.post(f"{KARAKEEP_API_ADDR}/api/v1/bookmarks", json=body, headers=headers, timeout=60)
    r.raise_for_status()
    bookmark = r.json()
    bid = bookmark["id"]

    wanted = list(dict.fromkeys([t.strip() for t in tags if t.strip()] + ["claude-sourced"]))
    tr = requests.post(
        f"{KARAKEEP_API_ADDR}/api/v1/bookmarks/{bid}/tags",
        json={"tags": [{"tagName": t} for t in wanted]},
        headers=headers,
        timeout=60,
    )
    tr.raise_for_status()

    # A list-scoped reader files the note into its own list so the ingest worker
    # routes it to this SME collection instead of lab_memory. List membership is
    # deterministic (no async tagger in the loop), so routing is decided at save.
    if LIST_ID:
        lr = requests.put(
            f"{KARAKEEP_API_ADDR}/api/v1/lists/{LIST_ID}/bookmarks/{bid}",
            headers=headers,
            timeout=60,
        )
        lr.raise_for_status()

    return {
        "id": bid,
        "tags": wanted,
        "list_id": LIST_ID,
        "citation": f"{PUBLIC_ADDR}/dashboard/preview/{bid}",
    }

lines: +        # Milvus returns matches sorted by COSINE score descending, so the first
+        # sub-floor hit means every remaining one is too -> stop.

content.text: Syntax check, commit, push, and regenerate the MCP source ConfigMap.

input.command:
cd /sessions/adoring-vibrant-keller/mnt/Developer/lab-memory
python3 -m py_compile mcp/src/lab_memory_mcp/retrieval.py && echo "retrieval.py OK"
# clear any stale locks (mount blocks unlink; rename aside)
for L in $(find .git -name '*.lock' 2>/dev/null); do mv "$L" "$L.stale.$$" 2>/dev/null; done
git checkout -- mcp/src/lab_memory_mcp/__pycache__/ 2>/dev/null
test -f .entire/settings.json && echo "entire: PRESENT" || echo "entire: MISSING"
export GIT_AUTHOR_NAME="darthzen" GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL="rick@theashfords.org" GIT_COMMITTER_NAME="darthzen" GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL="rick@theashfords.org"
git add mcp/src/lab_memory_mcp/retrieval.py
git commit -q -m "recall: add tunable relevance floor (RECALL_MIN_SCORE, default 0.25)

Live arctic-embed2 scores: noise tops ~0.20, weakest genuine paraphrase ~0.26.
Drop sub-floor hits (sorted desc, so break on first). Env-tunable per reader; 0 disables." 2>&1 | grep -viE 'tmp_obj|unable to unlink|Operation not permitted|Entire CLI' ; echo "commit rc=${PIPESTATUS[0]}"
# regenerate mcp-src configmap from updated source
python3 - <<'PY'
import yaml
data={k:open(f"mcp/src/lab_memory_mcp/{k}").read() for k in ("retrieval.py","server.py","__init__.py")}
obj={"apiVersion":"v1","kind":"ConfigMap","metadata":{"name":"lab-memory-mcp-src","namespace":"lab-memory"},"data":data}
open("/sessions/adoring-vibrant-keller/mnt/Developer/lab-memory-work/cm-mcp-src.yaml","w").write(yaml.safe_dump(obj,default_flow_style=False,width=100000,sort_keys=False))
print("regenerated cm-mcp-src.yaml")
PY
git log --oneline -1

{
  "query": "adapting your system while keeping core values constant",
  "count": 2,
  "results": [
    {
      "title": "popovich_leadership",
      "snippet": "a floor requirement.** Players not selected for the culture (or acquired mid-career without the trust runway) sometimes experienced the candor as pure harshness. The system's dependence on pre-screening is a limitation, not just a feature.
- **The self-promotion origin.** He got the job by firing his coach and installing himself while GM. The servant-leader reputation was built *after* a decidedly non-servant power move.
- **Structural advantages that don't transfer:** drafting Duncan first overall in 1997, near-unfireable job security, ownership that never meddled, and a small market with low media pressure. The style is real, but it grew in unusually favorable soil.

---

## 5. Synthesis: the operating model in one place

Stated as a system rather than a slogan list:

1. **Screen for coachability and low ego before anything else.** Development capacity is bounded by selection quality.
2. **Bank relational capital deliberately, in advance, at personal cost.** Dinners, cultural fluency, knowing families — the trust account gets funded before withdrawals begin.
3. **Spend that capital on maximum candor, aimed hardest at the most senior people.** Criticizing the star first makes criticism safe for everyone.
4. **Hold values constant; treat systems and schemes as fully disposable.** Adapt the *how* relentlessly, never the *what matters*.
5. **Optimize for the long horizon even when it costs in the short term** — rest, fines, rebuild years, developing assistants who will leave.
6. **Make succession an explicit output.** The coaching tree and the Hammon hire were investments in people whose success would accrue elsewhere.
7. **Treat people as whole humans on company time.** Curiosity about their lives is part of the job, not a distraction from it.
8. **Deflect credit outward with total consistency.** It compounds trust and keeps the ego screen credible.

**[INFERENCE — the one-sentence version]** Popovich's style is high standards fused with high, pre-paid care, ap",
      "citation": "https://karakeep.ash4d.com/dashboard/preview/ze9bfp1dx9awgeftr6fklr4s",
      "source_url": "https://karakeep.ash4d.com/dashboard/preview/ze9bfp1dx9awgeftr6fklr4s",
      "tags": "NBA,Leadership,Gregg Popovich,Coaching Philosophy,Sports Management,Organizational Culture",
      "score": 0.2931,
      "chunk": 6
    },
    {
      "title": "popovich_leadership",
      "snippet": "erred style of play as disposable and his standards as permanent. Most long-tenured leaders invert this — they keep the system and let the standards erode.

### 2.7 Developing whole people, on purpose

- Film sessions frequently **opened with discussion of current events, history, or documentaries** before any basketball. Players confirm this across eras.
- He pushed players to read, to vote, to have opinions, and to understand the world they lived in. He spoke openly and repeatedly on social justice, criticized sitting presidents by name, and donated heavily to causes including the San Antonio Food Bank and the Innocence Project — with the on-record explanation that "we're rich as hell, and we don't need it all."
- The wine obsession, the geopolitics, the food — he modeled being a complete adult with interests outside the job, and treated players' non-basketball selves as legitimate topics of team time.

**[INFERENCE]** This served retention and performance, not just principle. Players who felt seen as full humans stayed in San Antonio at below-market contracts with unusual frequency (Duncan, Parker, Ginóbili all took significant discounts). The whole-person investment had a measurable roster-construction payoff, whether or not that was the primary motive.

### 2.8 Organizational alignment and ego management upward

- The Popovich–R.C. Buford–Holt family triangle stayed intact for roughly three decades — an eternity in professional sports. No public front-office power struggles, no leaked blame, no contract-year drama from Popovich himself.
- He held GM-level or higher authority for most of his tenure, which is a structural fact worth being honest about: **his patience was possible partly because nobody could fire him for exercising it.** The model depends on job security most leaders do not have.

---

- In-game and postgame, Popovich was famously terse, sarcastic, and sometimes openly rude to reporters. On topics he ",

lastPrompt: We want good results. I definitely want a floor. Is 0.3 a high enough floor?

content.text:
Floor is live on all three readers and doing exactly what you want:

| Test | Before floor | After floor (0.25) |
|---|---|---|
| Popovich query → **parent** reader | MEDDIC noise @ 0.20 | **empty (0 results)** ✓ |
| Paraphrased "adapt system, hold values" → **child** | Popovich @ 0.29, 0.26 | **kept @ 0.29, 0.26** ✓ |
| Real MEDDIC query → parent | 0.53, 0.41 | **kept @ 0.53, 0.41** ✓ |

This is the proof that 0.25 was right and 0.3 would have been wrong: that 0.26 chunk is a genuine bullseye ("Hold values constant; treat systems disposable" is literally the document's point 4), and it survives with 0.01 to spare — a 0.3 floor would have silently dropped it.

So noise is gone, real hits at every relevance level are preserved. It's committed (`8728aa0`, pushed to darthzen) and deployed to the base reader plus both SME readers.

Because it's the `RECALL_MIN_SCORE` env var, you can tune per reader without a code change — I'd only revisit if you notice a pattern: bump toward 0.30 if junk still slips through as the corpus grows, or ease to 0.22 if good paraphrased queries start coming back empty. Recording the calibration so it's not guesswork next time.

input.old_string: Adding an SME domain = new top-level Karakeep list + SME_ROUTES entry + reader manifest.
input.new_string: Adding an SME domain = new top-level Karakeep list + SME_ROUTES entry + reader manifest. Recall relevance floor added 2026-07-15 (commit 8728aa0): `RECALL_MIN_SCORE` env, default 0.25, drops sub-floor hits (env-tunable per reader, 0 disables). Calibrated on live arctic-embed2 COSINE scores: pure noise ≤0.12, conceptual-bleed noise up to ~0.20, weakest GENUINE paraphrase matches ~0.26, close-wording ~0.5, direct ~0.63–0.72 — so 0.25 cuts noise while keeping real paraphrase hits (0.3 would have dropped legit 0.26 matches). arctic-embed2 compresses the score range; recalibrate as corpus grows. Verified: Popovich query on parent reader now returns empty (was 0.20 MEDDIC noise), paraphrase hits at 0.26/0.29 preserved on child.

lines: **P2 + P3 DONE 2026-07-12.** Ingest worker live: Secret `lab-memory-ingest` + ConfigMap `lab-memory-ingest-script` + CronJob `lab-memory-ingest` (*/15) in ns lab-memory; Milvus collection `lab_memory` (1024-d COSINE) holds 61 docs / 432 chunks, 0 failures. T-008 eval: **4/4 correct manual in top-3** (top chunk is sometimes the TOC — chunk-level precision is a P4 rerank problem). T-008 + T-009 CLOSED. lab-memory MCP built and running in-cluster (Deployment `lab-memory-mcp`, streamable-http :8765, ingress mcp-memory.ash4d.com/mcp, cert issuing) with tools `recall` / `save` / `memory_status` — verified initialize→tools/list→tools/call from inside the cluster. Repo commits 3a63a4e (worker hardening) + 67a1a0f (MCP). Bugs worth remembering: **Milvus VARCHAR max_length counts BYTES not chars** (smart quotes blew the 2048 preview cap); citation URLs must come from PUBLIC_ADDR, never the in-cluster svc addr; `client.query()` caps at 16384 rows so reconcile state needs `query_iterator`. Karakeep API v1 has NO reprocess endpoint — reprocessing image-only PDFs is a UI action. `OCR_USE_LLM=true` now live (patched into the StatefulSet + committed to values.yaml). Cluster is BEHIND the repo on the ingress: `karakeep-ash4d-tls` cert is issued and ready but the live Ingress has no TLS block — `deploy/install.sh` was never re-run after commit 6ac4e21, which is why Karakeep is still HTTP-only and T-004 stays blocked (now T-013). **MCP endpoint LIVE over HTTPS 2026-07-12:** `https://mcp-memory.ash4d.com/mcp` (streamable-http, no auth) — A record → 192.168.7.150, cert `mcp-memory-ash4d-tls` issued by letsencrypt-dns (valid to 2026-10-10), verified 200 on `initialize` through Traefik with a real LE chain. Add to Claude Desktop as a custom connector. **Cert-issuance lesson (supersedes the router-hijack theory for this case): a DNS-01 challenge stuck on "not yet propagated" for ~14 min was NXDOMAIN negative caching at the recursive resolvers, NOT the :53 hijack — proved the pod reaches 8.8.8.8/1.1.1.1 directly via an `o-o.myaddr.l.google.com` TXT probe (unforgeable by a middlebox), and the challenge then passed unattended. Wait 15 minutes before touching a hung DNS-01 challenge.** **T-013 DONE 2026-07-12: HTTPS live on https://karakeep.ash4d.com** (LE cert, valid to 2026-10-09; /api/health = 200), NEXTAUTH_URL + PUBLIC_ADDR both https, recall citations now https — and `retrieval.recall()` builds citations from PUBLIC_ADDR at query time, so host/scheme changes never require re-embedding. T-004 unblocked (Safari/iOS login was HTTPS-gated). **DRIFT WARNING: the TLS ingress + OCR_USE_LLM + NEXTAUTH_URL went in as kubectl patches (no helm reachable from the Cowork sandbox) — helm's stored release still lacks them, so a future `helm upgrade` WITHOUT running deploy/install.sh could revert TLS. deploy/values.yaml already carries all three; running install.sh converges it.** Full write→embed→recall loop verified through the HTTPS MCP endpoint (save → CronJob upsert → recall top hit). Open tickets: T-010 (fresh-session recall check — server side done), T-011 (Harbor images — both workloads run interim BCI+pip+ConfigMap), T-012 (LLM-OCR reprocess of 4 image-only PDFs), T-014 (auth posture for the unauthenticated MCP endpoint — `save` accepts writes from anyone on the LAN). Sandbox has NO LAN egress: all cluster work goes through the kubernetes MCP, and `kubectl_apply --filename` reads Mac paths (staging dir: ~/Developer/lab-memory-work/).

-**SME memory LIVE 2026-07-15.** Hierarchical, list-routed SME layer on top of lab_memory (design in [[karakeep-list-api]]). Committed to darthzen/lab-memory (c97a3f6 feature + b651a63 entire artifacts), main + entire/checkpoints/v1 pushed. Worker (`worker/ingest.py`) gained `SME_ROUTES=rootListId:collection`: fetches list tree + per-list membership, routes each bookmark under a routed subtree into a per-domain collection stamped `list_id` (deepest-wins), single-pass reconcile of lab_memory (complement) + every SME collection from one snapshot (no comingling, no inter-job race), pre-seeds configured SME collections. MCP (`mcp/src/lab_memory_mcp/`) gained `LIST_ID` scope: cached parent-map, `ancestor_chain`, recall filters `list_id in {self+ancestors}` (downward inheritance), `save()` files into LIST_ID for deterministic write-routing. Reader-per-list topology: `mcp/k8s/sme-salesengineer.yaml` = parent reader (LIST_ID=ipnsyy1t55vakjtzkn25y1p0, scope {self}) + child reader salesengineer-manager (LIST_ID=uxupn8cpjd78dezx14fzl4sc, scope {child,parent}), both COLLECTION=sme_salesengineer. DEPLOYED via kubernetes MCP: ConfigMaps lab-memory-ingest-script + lab-memory-mcp-src updated from repo, cronjob patched with SME_ROUTES, both SME reader Deployments/Services/Ingresses up, base lab-memory-mcp restarted. Verified: manual ingest created `sme_salesengineer` (1024-d COSINE, list_id field, rows=0), lab_memory untouched (540 chunks). DNS live for mcp-sme-salesengineer + mcp-sme-salesengineer-manager (certs will issue via letsencrypt-dns). Adding an SME domain = new top-level Karakeep list + SME_ROUTES entry + reader manifest.

+**SME memory LIVE 2026-07-15.** Hierarchical, list-routed SME layer on top of lab_memory (design in [[karakeep-list-api]]). Committed to darthzen/lab-memory (c97a3f6 feature + b651a63 entire artifacts), main + entire/checkpoints/v1 pushed. Worker (`worker/ingest.py`) gained `SME_ROUTES=rootListId:collection`: fetches list tree + per-list membership, routes each bookmark under a routed subtree into a per-domain collection stamped `list_id` (deepest-wins), single-pass reconcile of lab_memory (complement) + every SME collection from one snapshot (no comingling, no inter-job race), pre-seeds configured SME collections. MCP (`mcp/src/lab_memory_mcp/`) gained `LIST_ID` scope: cached parent-map, `ancestor_chain`, recall filters `list_id in {self+ancestors}` (downward inheritance), `save()` files into LIST_ID for deterministic write-routing. Reader-per-list topology: `mcp/k8s/sme-salesengineer.yaml` = parent reader (LIST_ID=ipnsyy1t55vakjtzkn25y1p0, scope {self}) + child reader salesengineer-manager (LIST_ID=uxupn8cpjd78dezx14fzl4sc, scope {child,parent}), both COLLECTION=sme_salesengineer. DEPLOYED via kubernetes MCP: ConfigMaps lab-memory-ingest-script + lab-memory-mcp-src updated from repo, cronjob patched with SME_ROUTES, both SME reader Deployments/Services/Ingresses up, base lab-memory-mcp restarted. Verified: manual ingest created `sme_salesengineer` (1024-d COSINE, list_id field, rows=0), lab_memory untouched (540 chunks). DNS live for mcp-sme-salesengineer + mcp-sme-salesengineer-manager (certs will issue via letsencrypt-dns). Adding an SME domain = new top-level Karakeep list + SME_ROUTES entry + reader manifest. Recall relevance floor added 2026-07-15 (commit 8728aa0): `RECALL_MIN_SCORE` env, default 0.25, drops sub-floor hits (env-tunable per reader, 0 disables). Calibrated on live arctic-embed2 COSINE scores: pure noise ≤0.12, conceptual-bleed noise up to ~0.20, weakest GENUINE paraphrase matches ~0.26, close-wording ~0.5, direct ~0.63–0.72 — so 0.25 cuts noise while keeping real paraphrase hits (0.3 would have dropped legit 0.26 matches). arctic-embed2 compresses the score range; recalibrate as corpus grows. Verified: Popovich query on parent reader now returns empty (was 0.20 MEDDIC noise), paraphrase hits at 0.26/0.29 preserved on child.

**Gotchas learned 2026-07-15:** (1) The `~/Developer` mount in the Cowork sandbox blocks `unlink()` on .git files — stale `*.lock` files can't be `rm`'d; rename them aside (`mv x.lock x.lock.stale`) since rename IS permitted, else git commits fail on lock creation. (2) The lab-memory remote is now SSH (git@github.com:darthzen/lab-memory.git) but the sandbox has no SSH host key — push over HTTPS with the darthzen PAT instead. (3) entire CLI is NOT installed in the Cowork sandbox, so the `.claude/settings.json` git hook self-skips ("Entire CLI enabled but not on PATH") on sandbox commits — commits are plain git (safe, pushed) but generate no entire checkpoints; commit from a machine with entire installed for checkpoint coverage. RECOVERY (worked 2026-07-15): Cowork stores each session transcript in native Claude Code format at `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/local-agent-mode-sessions/<acct>/<space>/local_<sessionid>/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/<uuid>.jsonl` (NOT ~/.claude; and `audit.jsonl` at the session root is Cowork's own log, not the transcript). Recover the session context into entire with `entire import claude-code --path "<dir containing that .jsonl>"` (run from the repo; --dry-run first). Imports as read-only checkpoints, not linked to the commits, but the prompts/transcript/reasoning are preserved + searchable.

content.text: That's the real clue: your local `entire/checkpoints/v1` is still at `7c2ded6` — identical to the remote, zero commits ahead. So there's **nothing new on that branch to push**; the import didn't land on it. Your `settings.json` had `"type": "git-refs"` — that's ref-based storage, where checkpoints live in individual git refs, not on the shared branch. Let me find where the imported data actually went.

input.command:
cd /sessions/adoring-vibrant-keller/mnt/Developer/lab-memory
echo "### all local refs mentioning 'entire' or 'checkpoint':"
git show-ref 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'entire|checkpoint' || echo "(none)"
echo
echo "### full ref namespace under refs/entire (if ref-based):"
git for-each-ref --format='%(refname) %(objecttype) %(committerdate:relative)' 'refs/entire/**' 2>/dev/null || echo "(no refs/entire/*)"
echo
echo "### any refs at all outside heads/remotes/tags:"
git for-each-ref --format='%(refname)' 2>/dev/null | grep -vE '^refs/(heads|remotes|tags)/' | head -30 || echo "(none)"
echo
echo "### remote refs on darthzen mentioning entire/checkpoint:"
git ls-remote "REDACTED" 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'entire|checkpoint' || echo "(none)"
echo
echo "### .entire local store contents:"
find .entire -maxdepth 3 2>/dev/null | head -40

lastPrompt: My data isn't showing in entire. Do we need to push the branch with the metadata?

content.text:
Found it — and it flips the answer. Good news first: **your data is imported and already on GitHub. No push needed.**

What's actually going on:

- Your backend is **ref-based** (`"type": "git-refs"`), so checkpoints are stored as individual git refs under `refs/entire/checkpoints/*`, **not** as commits on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch. That branch correctly stays at the `7c2ded6` init commit — that's normal for ref-based storage, not a sign anything's missing.
- The import **worked**: there are **18 checkpoint refs**, all created ~2 hours ago (your import of this session).
- They're **already pushed** — `git ls-remote` shows all 18 refs present on darthzen with matching SHAs. So there's nothing unpushed; a branch push would move nothing.

So why don't you see them? Almost certainly *where* you're looking, not *whether* they synced:

- **In the CLI:** imported checkpoints are read-only and **not linked to a git commit**, so they don't appear in commit/branch views like `entire checkpoint list`. They're searchable — find them with:

  ```bash
  cd ~/Developer/lab-memory
  entire checkpoint search "MEDDIC"
  entire search "SME memory list routing"
  ```

- **In entire.io (web app):** refs living on your GitHub remote don't automatically surface in the web UI — the repo has to be **connected/mirrored to entire.io** for that. Being on darthzen ≠ being in entire.io. Check `entire repo` / the mirror setup if that's where you're looking.

Let me confirm those refs really are this session's content so you know exactly what's there:

input.command:
cd /sessions/adoring-vibrant-keller/mnt/Developer/lab-memory
echo "### subject + date of 3 checkpoint refs:"
for r in refs/entire/checkpoints/04/36ca43e67604 refs/entire/checkpoints/50/11c4b9fe5950 refs/entire/checkpoints/f4/d57a7d727ef4; do
  git log -1 --format='%ci  %s' "$r" 2>/dev/null
done
echo
echo "### total imported checkpoint refs:"; git for-each-ref 'refs/entire/checkpoints/**' 2>/dev/null | wc -l
echo "### local vs remote parity (refs only local, not on remote):"
comm -23 <(git for-each-ref --format='%(objectname)' 'refs/entire/checkpoints/**' | sort) \
         <(git ls-remote "REDACTED" 'refs/entire/checkpoints/*' | awk '{print $1}' | sort) | head || echo "all local refs are on remote"

So to answer directly: **no, you don't need to push anything.** The metadata is ref-based and fully on GitHub; the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch sitting at the init commit is exactly how ref-based storage looks — the checkpoints live in `refs/entire/checkpoints/*`, not on that branch.

The data isn't missing, it's just not where you were looking. Two ways to surface it:

- **entire.io web app** — the refs being on your GitHub remote doesn't publish them to the hosted UI. That requires the repo to be connected/mirrored in entire.io (`entire repo` to check). If that's where you expected to see them, that connection is the missing piece — not a push.

If `entire search` returns them, you're fully done. If you're aiming for the web UI and it's still empty after confirming the repo is connected, tell me what `entire repo` reports and I'll dig in.
